does: This paper provides rigorous mathematical analysis of superposition in simple autoencoders with sparse inputs, complementing the empirical work of Elhage et al. (2022). The authors derive upper and lower bounds on L2 reconstruction loss for autoencoders with power activation functions, with bounds that are tight in the very sparse regime. The core contribution is establishing when and why neural networks optimally choose to represent multiple sparse features as non-orthogonal directions in lower-dimensional latent spaces rather than orthogonal ones, a phenomenon that explains polysemanticity in individual neurons.
connects: This work builds directly on the foundational empirical investigation by Elhage et al. (2022) on superposition as a mechanistic explanation for polysemanticity in neural networks. It connects to the broader interpretability literature attempting to understand feature representation and neuron function, extending earlier observations about neural network compression and the role of input sparsity. The paper engages with classical information-theoretic ideas about efficient coding and dimension reduction, bringing rigorous optimization and approximation theory to bear on questions previously explored mainly through empirical demonstrations and intuitive arguments about space utilization.
enables: By establishing rigorous bounds on when superposition emerges and becomes optimal, this work enables more precise theoretical understanding of the mechanistic interpretability landscape. It supports the development of better diagnostics for detecting superposition in real networks and provides foundations for predicting when interpretability becomes difficult. The tight bounds in sparse regimes offer tools for analyzing specific architectural and data properties where clean interpretability is or is not possible, potentially guiding design choices in interpretable-by-construction models. It also points toward understanding phase transitions in representation learning and informs theoretical frameworks for controllable feature organization in neural networks.
